pr_title: Fix dask performance issues in VIIRS L1B reader
pr_body:
The indexing logic for dask arrays is much simpler now and that don't need to be any performance penalities like the ones that were occuring in the viirs_l1b reader. The BT data has to be created/loaded from a look up table, essentially `nc['lut'][nc['bt_band_radiance_data']]`.

This PR also drops the flag_X attributes that were in the NetCDF files since satpy (EWA resampling at least) assumes that these flags mean the variable represents a category product and should not be averaged. This is not true for any of the VIIRS L1B data. The flag_meaning/values attributes were representing the fill values which were no long being used. Personally I think this goes against CF standards. You should have one and only one fill value. If you need to indicate different "states" of certain pixels then you add a mask/quality flag variable.

This is slightly less theoretically correct in the
instance_uri_registry case:

- request.route_path handles certain "special" params such as
  _script_url, _scheme, _host, _port, _query, and _anchor. However, we
  never use these in calls to instance_uri.

- request.route_path calls route.pregenerator, which allows Pyramid
  plugins to do fancy things like customize the routes generated
  according to the request. This is apparently used by e.g. Pylons
  subdomain support. However, we don't appear to use this either.

Allow autocomplete disabling in form choice widgets
pr_body:
Autocomplete with select2 and ajax is really nice and I use it a lot, but sometimes I do not want this behaviour because I want to limit the provided queryset for the selection widget and this features is globally enabled when you define `autocomplete_search_fields()`method in your model.

I made some changes to disable autocomplete whenever I want in an specific widget of a form:

```
class YourForm(forms.ModelForm):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(YourForm, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        plan = getattr(self.instance, 'plan', None)
        if plan:
            self.fields['place'].queryset = plan.places.all()  # We are providing a subset of Places
            self.fields['place'].autocomplete = False  # We easily disable the django-jet autocomplete feature
```

[this task](https://uktrade.atlassian.net/browse/ED-201)

# Usage
 - API clients (`directory-api-client`, `directory-sso-api-client`, `directory-api-client-sso`) will use `utils.RequestSigner` to add the signature to the request headers.
 - API endpoint views (`directory-api`, `directory-sso`) will use `permissions.SignaturePermission`, which delegates the task of checking the provided signature to `utils.RequestSignatureChecker`
 - Proxy views (`directory-ui-buyer`, `directory-sso-proxy`) will use  `utils.RequestSignatureChecker` to check the provided signature is valid, then generate a new one using `utils.RequestSigner`.

# signing and secrets:
- `utils.RequestSigner` uses `SIGNATURE_AUTH_OUTBOUND_SECRET` when generating signatures to be attached as a header to outbound requests.
- `utils.RequestSignatureChecker` uses `SIGNATURE_AUTH_INBOUND_SECRET` when checking the validity of the signature provided on incoming requests.
- proxies will need to both check the inbound signature is valid and then generate a new signature for the outbound request.

# BDD

When `directory-api-client` makes a request
Then `SIGNATURE_AUTH_OUTBOND_SECRET` is used for generating the signature

When `directory-api` receives a request
Then `SIGNATURE_AUTH_INBOUND_SECRET` is used for checking the signature

An implication of this is that FAB and FAS should have their `SIGNATURE_AUTH_OUTBOND_SECRET` env var set to the same value as API's `SIGNATURE_AUTH_INBOUND_SECRET`. This implication is true for all similar relationships too (sso with sso proxy, sso-profile with fab, etc).

When this library is implemented on sso and sso-proxy we will need to remove "double alice", and instead have sso-proxy generate a new signature.

pr_title: BUG: make select-row update when shifted
pr_body:
Say there were two selection: A and B. When removing A, Polymer does not
remove DOM for A but, rather, one for B. As a result, A will be updated
with parameter of B and it will need to be reinitialized including
binding to the storage.

When updated, not only should it refetch the list of runs, but also needs
to detach event listeners with the previous storage keys. If not done,
any change in hash will trigger the callback and set the value
incorrectly.

[mle] allow to use GetChildInfo method on restored children
pr_body:
Recently we have seen a random crashing of NCP.

After debugging I see that there is a problem with assert: 
`mle_router.cpp:4892: void ot::Mle::MleRouter::SignalChildUpdated(..): Assertion error == OT_ERROR_NONE failed.`

This was introduced in #2280 

So basically, whenever Router/Leader (NCP) has stored child in persistent storage, and after reboot previous child does not exist anymore, node tries to remove the child after stored child timeout.
This results in following calls: `MleRouter::RemoveNeighbor -> MleRouter::SignalChildUpdated -> MleRouter::GetChildInfo`

Currently `GetChildInfo `method returns `OT_ERROR_NOT_FOUND `for children with other than valid state. However in the scenario described above, the state of a child is either `kStateRestored `(rx-off) or `kStateChildUpdateRequest `(rx-on). This finally leads to assert.

To reproduce, just use spinel-cli to create the network. Then join one end-device (preferable with short child timeout e.g. 10s). Then turn-off NCP, turn-off ED and turn-on NCP again. After child timeout of the node, assert will be caught.

pr_title: IP: Add support to IP-in-IP in IPv6 layer
pr_body:
This pull request adds support for IP-in-IP in IP layer which is an addition to #887 

As a result - forwarding of MPL packets with multicast scope larger than realm-local is possible.
Right now i see that there is an open discussion `SPEC-659` about hop-limit field in the encapsulating header. I have choosen to use the default one (64), and not that from the encapsulated header. Also i believe following RFC2473 source address of the encapsulating header is selected using the default procedure taking ff03::fc as an input instead of for example ff05::1 (from inner header).

Below I have attached wireshark capture of MPL messages send to pre-subscribed (for test) address ff05::11 (which are encapsulated using ff03::fc ALL_MPL_FORWARDERS address).

![ipinip](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5144764/19765689/f66438b2-9c49-11e6-9621-0de706e1d45c.png)

Also #713 probably should be closed after this is merged.

pr_title: Remove otPlatFlash API's and move settings from core to examples.
pr_body:
The otPlatFlash is only needed by the internal otPlatSettings implementations. The otPlatSettings implementation does not belong in core OpenThread source as it is platform specific.  As such, this implementation has been moved to examples and the underpinning flash API's have been moved there as well and have been given a new prefix "utils" in place of "otPlatFlash".

This allows customers to more accurately identify the otPlat API's which they must implement to support OpenThread and to still have access to a implementation of the otPlatSettings API's which is deemed non-trivial.

Currently, no matter what debug level OpenThread configured, mbedTLS's debug level is the same `4`, i.e. `DEBUG`. Although OpenThread does filter out logs according to its own log level, setting fixed debug level of mbedTLS may use more CPU.

This PR simply sets the debug level of mbedTLS according to OpenThread's configured debug level.

pr_title: numpy: Provide concrete size aliases, test equivalence for `dtype(...).num`
pr_body:
Closes #1328 

See the linked issue for context.

Note that this is split into two commits:
1. Introduces a simple explicit test on dtypes to guarantee that they match for concrete dtypes, and then some assurances on platform-dependent sizes. (No changes to `numpy.h`)
2. Mirrors the concrete type aliases (e.g. `NPY_INT32`). This solves an issue where `py::dtype::of<int64_t>().attr("num")` did not match to NumPy's definition of `NPY_INT64`.

TBH, I can understand some hesitation for incorporating the second commit. However, I would like to see the first commit make it in - took a bit to understand this issue and debug.

pr_title: Properly report exceptions thrown during module initialization.
pr_body:
If an exception is thrown during module initialization, the
error_already_set destructor will try to call `get_internals()` *after*
setting Python's error indicator, resulting in a `SystemError: ...
returned with an error set`.

Fix that by temporarily stashing away the error indicator when
initializing the internals.

(I *think* any proper unit test would require creating a new module just for that purpose, and I'm not particularly familiar with the testing machinery used by pybind, so guidance would be welcome here.  But "it works for me"...)

xref #1113.

pr_title: Fixing gcc warnings when used with -pedantic switch
pr_body:
Hi,

Thanks for pybind11 it's pretty awesome. I use it at work and it improved my c++ to python workflow tremendously. I compile stuff with gcc on Linux using -pedantic flag. There were two places in the library itself that generated warnings when using this mode. They are related to conversion of function pointer to object pointer and back, apparently it's not ok on some platforms and so "ISO C++ forbids casting between pointer-to-function and pointer-to-object". It is however perfectly fine on platforms where `sizeof(void*) == sizeof(void(*)())`.

So that's what I fixed (just the symptom not the reason). I also fixed different warnings in example code, some extra ';' and variadic macro related stuff.

I'm not including changes to cmake file, since I'm not sure if you want to use pedantic flag in a general case.

Regards,

Kirill

pr_title: Pull default options from a resource's parent.
pr_body:
If a resource's options bag does not specify `protect` or `provider`,
pull a default value from the resource's parent.

In order to allow a parent resource to specify providers for multiple
resource types, component resources now accept an optional map from
package name to provider instance. When a custom resource needs a
default provider from its parent, it checks its parent provider bag for
an entry under its package. If a component resource does not have a
provider bag, its pulls a default from its parent.

These changes also add a `parent` field to `InvokeOptions` s.t. calls to
invoke can use the same behavior as resource creation w.r.t. providers.

Fixes #1735, #1736.

pr_title: [Config] Retrieve/save values with async context manager
pr_body:
### Type

- Enhancement

### Description of the changes
Allows the use of an async context manager to get and set config values.

This works like so:
- Entering the context manager gets the value
- Exiting the context manager sets it.

This can be classed as both a stylistic enhancement as well as a functional one - the functional enhancement being inherent from the behaviour of context managers - if any exception occurs from within the context manager, or a the function returns, config will attempt to set the value as per any modifications which had been made.

Also important to note is that this will only work with mutable types such as `list` or `dict`. The context manager can only keep track of modifications to the value by referencing its address in memory, so keep that in mind. For this reason the context manager will raise a `TypeError` if a non-list or -dict is returned by the value getter.

### Examples

Appending a value to a list:
```py
async with self.config.list_of_foo() as list_of_foo:
    list_of_foo.append("foo")
```

The following will raise a `TypeError` upon entering the context manager, and not save any changes:
```py
async with self.config.my_int() as my_int:
    my_int += 1
```

The following code will *not* save the modifications to `list_of_foo` since its reference has been changed, and it was not modified in place:
```py
async with self.config.list_of_foo() as list_of_foo:
    list_of_foo = list_of_foo + ["foo"]
```

pr_title: make as_list work for all queries
Instanciate a single bootstrap object per client, add IP tracking
pr_body:
You can specify a callback as `__connect_handler__` class attribute on your bootstrap object (defaults to calling `on_connect` if it's present and callable) to track the user IP and port in case you want/need to. This implies having a single bootstrap object per client, which breaks backwards compatibility (but as I understand it, the proper C++ way is also to instance a bootstrap object for each client).

Feel free to not merge this - it's mostly here as "documentation" for people who need the feature to get an idea how to reach it. It's very possibly still wrong and/or buggy in some places.

Usage is demonstrated on https://github.com/Cheaterman/capnchat/ - I feel like it serves a good purpose as working example (as opposed to the tests and examples we currently have in the codebase - although the Calculator is useful, it cannot demonstrate how multiple clients would interact for instance).

Sorry for not taking the time to adapt a similar system to restorer and to socket-based instantiations of TwoPartyServer. I feel like these two features are kind of deprecated and missing some work (respectively), and I'll instead work on making connection strings work with UDP (possibly using udp:// prefix or something similar) - but that's for another PR.

Thanks for reading! Hope you like this!
